turns.text: In Simferopol the army command requested the Einsatzkommandos in its area to hasten the liquidations, because famine was threatening and there was a great housing shortage.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you know how many persons were liquidated by Einsatz Group D under your direction?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In the year between June 1941 to June 1942 the Einsatzkommandos reported 90,000 people liquidated.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did that include men, women, and children?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: On what do you base those figures?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: On reports sent by the Einsatzkommandos to the Einsatzgruppen.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Were those reports submitted to you?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you saw them and read them?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I beg your pardon?

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you saw and read those reports, personally?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And it is on those reports that you base the figures you have given the Tribunal?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you know how those figures compare with the number of persons liquidated by other Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The figures which I saw of other Einsatzgruppen are considerably larger.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: That was due to what factor?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I believe that to a large extent the figures submitted by the other Einsatzgruppen were exaggerated.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you see reports of liquidations from the other Einsatz groups from time to time?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And those reports showed liquidations exceeding those of Group D; is that correct?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you personally supervise mass executions of these individuals?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I was present at two mass executions for purposes of inspection.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you explain to the Tribunal in detail how an individual mass execution was carried out?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: A local Einsatzkommando attempted to collect all the Jews in its area by registering them. This registration was performed by the Jews themselves.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: On what pretext, if any, were they rounded up?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: On the pretext that they were to be resettled.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you continue?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: After the registration the Jews were collected at one place; and from there they were later transported to the place of execution, which was, as a rule an antitank ditch or a natural excavation. The executions were carried out in a military manner, by firing squads under command.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In what way were they transported to the place of execution?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: They were transported to the place of execution in trucks, always only as many as could be executed immediately. In this way it was attempted to keep the span of time from the moment in which the victims knew what was about to happen to them until the time of their actual execution as short as possible.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Was that your idea?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And after they were shot what was done with the bodies?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The bodies were buried in the antitank ditch or excavation.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What determination, if any, was made as to whether the persons were actually dead?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The unit leaders or the firing-squad commanders had orders to see to this and, if need be, finish them off themselves.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And who would do that?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Either the unit leader himself or somebody designated by him.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In what positions were the victims shot?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Standing or kneeling.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What was done with the personal property and clothing of the persons executed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: All valuables were confiscated at the time of the registration or the rounding up and handed over to the Finance Ministry, either through the RSHA or directly. At first the clothing was given to the population, but in the winter of 1941-42 it was collected and disposed of by the NSV.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: All their personal property was registered at the time?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, not all of it, only valuables were registered.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What happened to the garments which the victims were wearing when they went to the place of execution?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: They were obliged to take off their outer gar meets immediately before the execution.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: All of them?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The outer garments, yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How about the rest of the garments they were wearing?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The other garments remained on the bodies.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Was that true of not only your group but of the other Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That was the order in my Einsatzgruppe. I don't know how it was done in other Einsatzgruppen.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In what way did they handle it?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Some of the unit leaders did not carry out the liquidation in the military manner, but killed the victims singly by shooting them in the back of the neck.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And you objected to that procedure?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I was against that procedure, yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: For what reason?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Because both for the victims and for those who carried out the executions, it was, psychologically, an immense burden to bear.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, what was done with the property collected by the Einsatzkommandos from these victims?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: All valuables were sent to Berlin, to the RSHA or to the Reich Ministry of Finance. The articles which could be used in the operational area, were disposed of there.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: For example, what happened to gold and silver taken from the victims?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That was, as I have just said, turned over to Berlin, to the Reich Ministry of Finance.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How do you know that?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I can remember that it was actually handled in that way from Simferopol.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How about watches, for example, taken from the victims?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: At the request of the Army, watches were made available to the forces at the front.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Were all victims, including the men, women, and children, executed in the same manner?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Until the spring of 1942, yes. Then an order came from Himmler that in the future women and children were to be killed only in gas vans.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How had the women and children been killed previously?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In the same way as the men-by shooting.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What, if anything, was done about burying the victims after they had been executed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The Kommandos filled the graves to efface the signs of the execution, and then labor units of the population leveled them.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Referring to the gas vans which you said you received in the spring of 1942, what order did you receive with respect to the use of these vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: These gas vans were in future to be used for the killing of women and children.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you explain to the Tribunal the construction of these vans and their appearance?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The actual purpose of these vans could not be seen from the outside. They looked like closed trucks, and were so constructed that at the start of the motor, gas was conducted into the van causing death in 10 to 15 minutes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Explain in detail just how one of these vans was used for an execution.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The vans were loaded with the victims and driven to the place of burial, which was usually the same as that used for the mass executions. The time needed for transportation was sufficient to insure the death of the victims.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How were the victims induced to enter the vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: They were told that they were to be transported to another locality.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How was the gas turned on?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I am not familiar with the technical details.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How long did it take to kill the victims ordinarily? OHLENDORF About 10 to 15 minutes; the victims were not conscious of what was happening to them.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How many persons could be killed simultaneously in one such van?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: About 15 to 25 persons. The vans varied in size.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you receive reports from those persons operating these vans from time to time?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I didn't understand the question.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did you receive reports from those who were working on the vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I received the report that the Einsatzkommandos did not willingly use the vans.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Why not?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Because the burial of the victims was a great ordeal for the members of the Einsatzkommandos.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, will you tell who furnished these vans to the Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The gas vans did not belong to the motor pool of the Einsatzgruppen but were assigned to the Einsatzgruppe as a special unit, headed by the man who had constructed the vans. The vans were assigned to the Einsatzgruppen by the RSHA.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Were the vans supplied to all of the different Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I am not certain of that. I know only in the case of Einsatzgruppe D, and indirectly that Einsatzgruppe C also made use of these vans.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Are you familiar with the letter from Becker to Rauff with respect to these gas vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I saw this letter during my interrogation.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: May it please the Tribunal, I am referring to Exhibit 501-PS, Exhibit USA-288, being a letter already in evidence, a letter from Becker to Rauff. [Turning to the witness.] Will you tell the Tribunal who Becker was?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: According to my recollection, Becker was the constructor of the vans. It was he who was in charge of the vans of Einsatzgruppe D.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Who was Rauff?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Rauff was group leader in Amt II of the RSHA. Among other things, he was at that time in charge of transportation.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Can you identify that letter in any way?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The contents roughly correspond to my experiences and are therefore probably correct. [Document 501-PS was handed to the witness.]

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you look at the letter you and tell us whether you can identify it in any way?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The external appearance of the letter as well as the initial "R" (Rauff) on it, and the reference to Zwabel or Fabe: who took care of transportation under Rauff, seems to testify to the letter's authenticity. The contents roughly correspond to the experiences which I had at that time.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: So that you believe it to be an authentic document,

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you now lay it aside on the table there? Referring to your previous testimony, will you explain to the Tribunal why you believe that the type of execution ordered by you, namely, military, was preferable to the shooting-in-the-neck procedure adopted by the other Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: On the one hand, the aim was that the individual leaders and men should be able to carry out the executions in a military manner acting on orders and should not have to make a decision of their own; it was, to all intents and purposes, an order which they were to carry out. On the other hand, it was known to me that through the emotional excitement of the executions ill-treatment could not be avoided, since the victims discovered too soon that they were to be executed and could not therefore endure prolonged nervous strain. And it seemed intolerable to me that individual leaders and men should in consequence be forced to kill a large number of people on their own decision.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: In what manner did you determine which were the Jews to be executed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That was not part of my task; but the identification of the Jews was carried out by the Jews themselves, since the registration was handled by a Jewish Council of Elders.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Did the amount of Jewish blood have anything to do with it?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I can't remember the details, but I believe that half-Jews were also considered as Jews.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What organizations furnished most of the officer personnel of the Einsatz groups and Einsatzkommandos?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I did not understand the question.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What organizations furnished most of the officer personnel of the Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The officer personnel was furnished by the State Police, the Kripo, and, to a lesser extent, by the SD.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Kripo?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, the State Police, the Criminal Police and, to a lesser extent, the SD.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Were there any other sources of personnel?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, most of the men employed were furnished by the Waffen-SS and the Ordnungspolizei. The State Police and the Kripo furnished most of the experts, and the troops were furnished by the Waffen-SS and the Ordnungspolizei.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How about the Waffen-SS?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The Waffen-SS and the Ordnungspolizei were each supposed to supply the Einsatzgruppen with one company.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: How about the Order Police?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The Ordnungspolizei also furnished the Einsatzgruppen with one company.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: What was the size of Einsatz Group D and its operating area as compared with the other Einsatz groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I estimate that Einsatzgruppe D was one-half or two-thirds as large as the other Einsatzgruppen. That changed in the course of time, since some of the Einsatzgruppen were greatly enlarged.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: May it please the Tribunal, I have other questions relating to organizational matters which I think would clarify some of the evidence which has already been in part received by the Tribunal; but I don't want to take the time of the Tribunal unless they feel that they want any more such testimony. I thought perhaps if any members of the Tribunal had questions they would ask this witness directly, because he is the best informed on these organizational matters of anyone who will be presented in Court.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will adjourn now for 10 minutes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Colonel Amen, the Tribunal does not think that it is necessary to go further into the organizational questions at this stage, but it is a matter which must be really decided by you because you know what the nature of the evidence which you are considering is. So far as the Tribunal is concerned, they are satisfied at the present stage to leave the matter where it is. But there is one aspect of the witness' evidence which the Tribunal would like you to investigate, and that is whether the practices of which he has been speaking continued after 1942, and for how long.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: [To the witness] Can you state whether the liquidation practices which you have described continued after 1942 and, if so, for how long a period of time thereafter? 3 Jan.46

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I don't think that the basic order was ever revoked. But I cannot remember the details-at least not with regard to Russia-which would enable me to make concrete statements on this subject. The retreat began very shortly thereafter, so that the operational region of the Einsatzgruppen became ever smaller. I do know, however, that other Einsatzgruppen with similar orders had been envisaged for other areas.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Your personal knowledge extends up to what date?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I know that the liquidation of Jews was prohibited about six months before the end of the war. I also saw a document terminating the liquidation of Soviet commissars, but I cannot recall a specific date.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Do you know whether in fact it was so terminated?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, I believe so.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The Tribunal would like to know the number of men in your Einsatz group.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: There were about 500 men in my Einsatzgruppe, excluding those who were added to the group as assistants from the country itself.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Including them, did you say?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Excluding those who were added to the group from the country itself.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do you know how many there would be in other groups?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I estimate that at the beginning there were seven to eight hundred men; but, as I said, this number changed rapidly in the course of time, since the Einsatzgruppen themselves acquired new people or succeeded in getting additional personnel from the RSHA.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The numbers increased, did they?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, the numbers increased.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Now, here are perhaps just a half dozen of these questions I would like to ask, because I do think they might clear up, in the minds of the Tribunal, some of the evidence which has gone before. I shall be very brief, if that is satisfactory to the Tribunal. [Turning to the witness.] Will you explain the significance of the different widths of the blue lines on the chart?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The thick blue line between the position of Himmler as Reichsfuehrer SS and Chief of the German Police and the RSHA is designed to show the identity of the offices of the chiefs of the Sicherheitspolizei and the SD in their tasks. The RSHA treated both ministerial questions of leadership and individual executive questions, that is to say, matters of the Sipo and the SD. From the legal administrative point of view, however, the organizational scheme shows an illegal state of affairs in that the RSHA as such never actually had official validity. The formal, legal position was different from that shown on this chart. Party and State offices different authority were amalgamated. Under this designation RSHA, no directives or laws or orders could be issued on a legal basis, because the State Police, in its ministerial capacity, was still subordinate to the Ministry of the Interior, whereas the SD, despite this set-up, was an organ of the Party. Therefore if I wanted to reproduce this administrative scheme accurately, I should, for example, have to put in place of Amt IV, the Amt "Political Police", a part of the former Hauptamt Sicherheitspolizei. This Amt "Political Police" existed formally to the very end and had sprung from the Police Department of the Ministry of the Interior. Also, the Secret State Police Amt. the Central Office of the Prussian Secret State Police, the head office of all the political police offices of the different Lander, continued to exist formally. Thus, ministerial questions continued to be dealt with under the heading of the Minister of the Interior. So far as it was necessary to emphasize the formal competence of the Ministry of the Interior, this was indicated in the heading "Reich Minister of the Interior" with the filing notice "Pol," the former designation of the Police Department of the Ministry of the Interior, together with the appropriate filing notice of the competent department of the former Hauptamt Sicherheitspolizei; for example, filing notice "Pol-S" meant Sicherheitspolizei; "V" meant Amt Verwaltung und Recht (Administration and Law). The RSHA was therefore nothing more than a camouflage designation which did not correctly represent the actual state of affairs but gave the Chief of the Sipo and the SD, as a collective designation for the Chief of the Hauptamt Sicherheitspolizei and the Chief of the SD Hauptamt (an office which existed until 1939), the opportunity of using one or the other letterhead at any time. At the same time it gave him the opportunity of an internal amalgamation of all forces and the opportunity of a division of the spheres of work on a practical, effective basis. But the State offices in this Amt did remain in a way dependent on the Ministry of the Interior, and similarly the departments of the SD remained departments of the Party. The SD Hauptamt, or the RSHA, had formally only the significance of an SS Main Office, in which the SS members of the Sipo and the SD belonged to the SS. But the SS, that is to say, Himmler, as Reichsfuehrer SS, gave these State offices no official authority to issue orders.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am not sure that I follow altogether what you have been saying; but is what you have been saying the reason why you are shown on the chart as concerned with Amt m, which refers, apparently, only to inside Germany, while, according to your evidence, you were the head of Einsatz Group D, which was operating outside Germany?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The fact that I led an Einsatzgruppe had nothing to do with my position as Chief of Amt III. I led the Einsatzgruppe as an individual and not as Chief of Amt III; and in my capacity as leader of an Einsatzgruppe, I entered into a completely new function and assumed an office completely separate from my previous one.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I see. And did it involve that you left Germany and went into the area invaded in the Soviet Union?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you now explain the significance of the dotted blue lines, as compared with the solid blue lines on the right-hand side of the chart?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The solid lines indicate a direct official channel for orders, whereas the dotted lines signify that there was, as a rule, no direct channel.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Was the term "SD" ever used to include both the Sipo and the SD?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In the course of years the term "SD" was used more and more incorrectly. It came to be established as an abbreviation for Sipo and SD, without actually being suitable for that. "SD" was originally simply a designation for the fact that someone belonged to the SS through the SD Main Office. When the SD Main Office was dissolved and was taken over into the RSHA, the question arose whether the designation SD, which was also worn as insignia on the sleeve of the particular SS man, should be replaced by another insignia or a new abbreviation, e.g., RSHA. That was not done because the camouflage of the RSHA would thereby have been endangered. But when, for example, I read in a Fuehrer order that in France people were to be turned over to the SD, that was a case in point of the false use of the designation SD, because there were no such offices in France and the SD, insofar as it functioned in departments, e.g., Amt III, had no executive power but was purely an intelligence organ.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Briefly, what was the relationship between the SS and the Gestapo?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The relationship between the SS and Gestapo was this: The Reichsfuehrer SS, as such, took over the tasks of the Police and attempted to link the State Police and the SS more closely, that is to say, on the one hand to employ only members of the State Police who were eligible for the SS and, on the other hand, to use the institutions of the SS, e.g., education and training of the younger generation by the Waffen-SS, in order in this way to supply recruits for the State Police. This amalgamation was later extended by Himmler in an attempt to bring about the same relationship between the SS and the Ministry of the Interior, i.e., the whole internal administration.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: About how many full-time agents and honorary auxiliary personnel did the SD employ?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, well, in this connection, too, one cannot use the term SD; one must distinguish here between Amt III and Amt VI. Amt III, as the interior intelligence service, had about 3,000 salaried members, including men and women. On the other hand, the interior intelligence service worked essentially with honorary members, that is, with men and women who put their professional experiences and the experiences in their surroundings at the disposal of the interior intelligence service. I would judge their number to be roughly 30,000.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: Will you briefly give the Tribunal a general example of how a typical transaction was handled through the channels indicated on the chart?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: First, a general example, invented to make things clear. Himmler heard that more and more saboteurs were being dropped from planes into Germany and were endangering transportation and factory sites. He informed Kaltenbrunner in the latter's capacity as Chief of the Sipo and instructed him to draw the attention of his organs to this state of affairs and to take measures ensuring that these saboteurs would be seized as soon and as completely as possible. Kaltenbrunner instructed the chief of Amt IV, that is, the State Police, to prepare an order to this effect for the regional offices. This order was drawn up by the competent authorities in Amt IV and was either transmitted by Muller directly to the State Police offices in the Reich or-and this is more probable on account of the importance of the question and the necessity to bring the order at the same time to the attention of the other offices of the Sicherheitspolizei-or he gave it to Kaltenbrunner, who signed it and sent it to the regional offices in the Reich. An order of this sort laid down, for example, that the States Police offices were to report the measures they were taking as well as their results. These reports went back through the same 3 Jan.46 channels from the regional offices to the competent authorities in Amt IV, from there to the Chief of Amt IV, from there to the Chief of the Sicherheitspolizei, Kaltenbrunner, and then to the Chief of the German Police Himmler.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: And, finally will you give a specific example of a typical transaction handled through the channels indicated on the chart?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I take the example of the arrest of the leaders of the leftist parties after the events of the 20th of July: This order was also transmitted from Himmler to Kaltenbrunner; Kaltenbrunner passed it on to Amt IV and an appropriate draft for a decree was formulated by Amt IV, signed by Kaltenbrunner, and sent to the regional offices. The reports were returned from the subordinate offices back to the higher offices along the same channels.

turns.speaker: COL. AMEN
turns.text: May it please the Tribunal. The witness is now available to other counsel. I understand that Colonel Pokrovsky has some questions that he wishes to ask on behalf of the Soviets.

turns.speaker: COLONEL Y. V. POKROVSKY (Deputy Chief Prosecutor for the U.S.S.R.)
turns.text: The testimony of the witness is important for the clarification of questions in a report on which the Soviet Delegation is at present working. Therefore, with the permission of the Tribunal I would like to put a number of questions to the witness. [Turning to the witness.] Witness, you said that you were present twice at the mass executions. On whose orders were you an inspector at the executions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I was present at the executions on my own initiative.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: But you said that you attended as inspector.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I said that I attended for inspection purposes.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: On your initiative?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Did one of your chiefs always attend the executions for purposes of inspection?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Whenever possible I sent a member of the staff of the Einsatzgruppe to witness the executions, but this was not always feasible since the Einsatzgruppen had to operate over great distances.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Why was some person sent for purposes of inspection?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Would you please repeat the question?

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: For what purpose was an inspector sent?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: To determine whether or not my instructions regarding the manner of the execution were actually being carried out.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Am I to understand that the inspector was to make certain that the execution had actually been carried out?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, it would not be correct to say that. He was to ascertain whether the conditions which I had set for the execution were actually being carried out.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: What manner of conditions had you in mind?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: 1. Exclusion of the public; 2. Military execution by a firing-squad; 3. Arrival of the transports and carrying out of the liquidation in a smooth manner to avoid unnecessary excitement; 4. Supervision of the property to prevent looting. There may have been other details which I no longer remember. At any rate, all ill-treatment, whether physical or mental, was to be prevented through these measures.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You wished to make sure that what you considered to be an equitable distribution of this property was effected, or did you aspire to complete acquisition of the valuables?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You spoke of ill-treatment. What did you mean by ill-treatment at the executions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: If, for instance, the manner in which the executions were carried out caused excitement and disobedience among the victims, so that the Kommandos were forced to restore order by means of violence.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: What do you mean by "restore order by means of violence"? What do you mean by suppression of the excitement amongst the victims by means of violence?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: If, as I have already said, in order to carry out the liquidation in an orderly fashion it was necessary, for example, to resort to beating.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Was it absolutely necessary to beat the victims?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I myself never witnessed it, but I heard of it.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: From whom?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In conversations with members of other Kommandos. * 0nly the first half of the preceding question, originally spoken in Russian, was transmitted to the witness in German by the interpreter. The answer of the witness, therefore, refers only to this fist half of the question. Back 3 Jan.46

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You said that cars, autocars, were used for the executions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Do you know where, and with whose assistance, the inventor, Becker, was able to put his invention into practice?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I remember only that it was done through Amt II of the RSHA; but I can no longer say that with certainty.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: How many were executed in these cars? OHLENDORF; I did not understand the question.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: How many persons were executed by means of these cars?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot give precise figures, but the number was comparatively very small-perhaps a few hundred.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You said that mostly women and children were executed in these vans. For what reason?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That was a special order from Himmler to the effect that women and children were not to be exposed to the mental strain of the executions; and thus the men of the Kommandos, mostly married men, should not be compelled to aim at women and children.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Did anybody observe the behavior of the persons executed in these vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, the doctor.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Did you know that Becker had reported that death in these vans was particularly agonizing?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No. I learned of Becker's reports for the first time from the letter to Rauff, which was shown to me here. On the contrary, I know from the doctor's reports that the victims were not conscious of their impending death.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Did any military units-I mean, Army units-take part in these mass executions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: As a rule, no.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: And as an exception?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I think I remember that in Nikolaiev and in Simferopol a spectator from the Army High Command was present for a short time.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: For what purpose?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I don't know, probably to obtain information personally.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Were military units assigned to carry out the executions in these towns?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Officially, the Army did not assign any units for this purpose; the Army as such was actually opposed to the liquidation.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: But in practice?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Individual units occasionally volunteered. However, at the moment I know of no such case among the Army itself, but only among the units attached to the Army (Heeresgefolge).

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You were the man by whose orders people were sent to their death. Were Jews only handed over for the execution by the Einsatzgruppe or were Communists-"Communist officials" you call them in your instructions-handed over for execution along with the Jews?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, activists and political Commissary. Mere membership in the Communist Party was not sufficient to persecute or kill a man.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Were any special investigations made concerning the part played by persons in the Communist Party?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, I said on the contrary that mere membership of the Communist Party was not, in itself, a determining factor in persecuting or executing a man; he had to have a special political function.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Did you have any discussions on the murder vans sent from Berlin and on their use?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I did not understand the question.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Had you occasion to discuss, with your chiefs and your colleagues, the fact that motor vans had been sent to your own particular Einsatzgruppe from Berlin for carrying out the executions? Do you remember any such discussions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I do not remember any specific discussion.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Had you any information concerning the fact that members of the execution squad in charge of the executions were unwilling to use the vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I knew that the Einsatzkommandos were using these vans.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: No, I had something else in mind. I wanted to know whether you received reports that members of the execution squads were unwilling to use the vans and preferred other means of execution?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That they would rather kill by means of the gas vans than by shooting?

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: On the contrary, that they preferred execution by shooting to killing by means of the gas vans. 3 Jan.46

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, I have already said that the gas van...

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: And why did they prefer execution by shooting to killing in the gas vans?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Because, as I have already said, in the opinion of the leader of the Einsatzkommandos, the unloading of the corpses was an unnecessary mental strain.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: What do you mean by "an unnecessary mental strain"?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: As far as I can remember the conditions at that time-the picture presented by the corpses and probably because certain functions of the body had taken place leaving the corpses lying in filth.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: You mean to say that the sufferings endured prior to death were clearly visible on the victims? Did I understand you correctly?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I don't understand the question; do you mean during the killing in the van?

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I can only repeat what the doctor told me, that the victims were not conscious of their death in the van.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: In that case your reply to my previous question, that the unloading of the bodies made a very terrible impression on the members of the execution squad, becomes entirely incomprehensible.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: And, as I said, the terrible impression created by the position of corpses themselves, and by the state of the vans which had probably been dirtied and so on.

turns.speaker: COL. POKROVSKY
turns.text: I have no further questions to put to this witness at the present stage of the Trial.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does the Prosecutor for the French Republic desire to put any questions to the witness?

turns.speaker: M. FRANCOIS DE MENTHON (Chief Prosecutor for the French Republic)
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does the counsel for Kaltenbrunner desire to cross-examine now or at a later date?

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Perhaps I could ask a few questions now and request that I be allowed to make my cross-examination later, after consultation with Kaltenbrunner.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well. DR. KAUFFMANN [Turning to the witness.] : How long have you known Kaltenbrunner?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: May I be allowed to sit? May I sit down?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I saw Kaltenbrunner for the first time during the journey from Berlin to Himmler's headquarters at the time when Kaltenbrunner was to be appointed Chief of the Sipo and SD. Before that, I only knew of his existence.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: You did not know him?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I only knew of his existence.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Did you come into personal contact with Kaltenbrunner in private or official discussions after his appointment as Chief of the RSHA?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, of course.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know his views on the Jewish question, for example?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, I don't know his particular views on this question.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know his attitude in the church question?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In the question of the church-he repudiated the anti-church course followed in Germany. We agreed that an understanding had to be reached with the church.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know his attitude on the liquidation of civilian prisoners, parachutists, and so on?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know that Kaltenbrunner made special efforts to use the SD, in order to supply the criticism lacking at the Fuehrerstab?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, that was the task of the SD even before Kaltenbrunner, and he also gave his material support to this task.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: A little bit more slowly.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: It was the task of the SD even before Kaltenbrunner came, and he approved and materially supported this tendency.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know, either directly or indirectly, that Kaltenbrunner had no authority to give executive orders, for example, that he had no authority to send people to concentration camps or release them from concentration camps, that all these things were handled exclusively by Himmler and Muller?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I think this question is too general to be answered in a concrete way, it must be divided up. If you ask whether Kaltenbrunner could order executive actions, I must answer in the affirmative. If you then name Himmler and Muller to the exclusion of Kaltenbrunner, I must point out that in the organization of the RSHA Muller was Kaltenbrunner's subordinate; and consequently orders from Himmler to Muller were also orders to Kaltenbrunner, and Muller was obliged to inform Kaltenbrunner of them. On the other hand, it is certain that, particularly in regard to the concentration camps, the final decision on dispatch to them or release from them was really made by Himmler. I can say with absolute certainty-in this connection the expression "to the last washerwoman" was often used-that Himmler reserved the final decision for himself. Whether Kaltenbrunner had any authority at all in this regard, I cannot say definitely.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Did you personally see the original orders and original signatures of Kaltenbrunner ordering the liquidation of sabotage troops and so on?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Do you know, either directly or indirectly, that after Heydrich's death a change, which to be sure was not a formal change, took place and that another and milder course was followed by Kaltenbrunner?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I couldn't answer that question with concrete proof.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Then I will leave that question, and come to another. Did Kaltenbrunner know that you were an Einsatz leader in the East?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Who gave you this order?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Heydrich.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: Heydrich gave it to you? Then it was before this time?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, of course.

turns.speaker: DR. KAUFFMANN
turns.text: I have no further questions at the moment.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Major General I. T. Nikitchenko, Member for the U.S.S.R.)
turns.text: Witness Ohlendorf, can you answer up to what date the Einsatzgruppe under your command was operating?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The staff of the Einsatzgruppe went as far as the Caucasus and then returned. As far as I can remember, a combat command (Kampfkommando) was formed out of it under the name "Bierkamp," and that was used in fighting the partisans. Then, I think, the Einsatzgruppe was entirely disbanded, Bierkamp went into the Government General and took a large number of his men with him.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: What did the group do after Bierkamp left?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I think I can say that the Einsatzgruppe ceased to exist after the retreat from the Caucasus. It took over tasks similar to those of the Wehrmacht under the immediate command of the Commander of the Sicherheitspolizei in the Ukraine and particularly under the command of the Higher SS and Police Leaders in the Ukraine.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: In other words, it merely carried out its activities in different surroundings under different leadership, and that was all the difference. Such functions as were performed by the Einsatzgruppe in the past continued to be carried out in new surroundings.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, it actually became a combat unit.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: What does that mean? Against whom were the military actions directed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Within the scope of operations directed against the partisan movement.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Can you say more particularly what this group was actually doing?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: After the retreat?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: .When you say that the function of this group changed when it conducted operations against the partisans.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I have no concrete experiences myself. It was probably used for reconnaissance against the partisans and also in combat.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: But did it carry out any executions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I can no longer say that definitely for this period, for the unit now entered territories in which that sort of activity was out of the question.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: In your testimony you said that the Einsatz group had the object of annihilating the Jews and the commissars, is that correct?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: And in what category did you consider the children? For what reason were the children massacred?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The order was that the Jewish population should be totally exterminated.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Including the children? 3 Jan.46

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Were all the Jewish children murdered?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: But the children of those whom you considered as belonging to the category of commissars, were they also killed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I am not aware that inquiries were ever made after the families of Soviet commissars.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Did you send anywhere reports on the executions which the group carried out?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Reports on the executions were regularly submitted to the RSHA.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: No, did you personally send any reports on the annihilation of thousands of people which you effected? Did you personally submit any report?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The reports came from the Einsatzkommandos who carried out the actions, to the Einsatzgruppe and the Einsatzgruppe informed the RSHA.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Whom?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The reports went to the Chief of the Sipo personally.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Personally?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, personally.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: What was the name of this police officer? Can you give his name?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: At that time, Heydrich.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: After Heydrich? .

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I was no longer there then, but that was the standing order.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: I am asking you whether you continued to submit reports after Heydrich's death or not?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: After Heydrich's death I was no longer in the Einsatz, but the reports were, of course, continued.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Do you know whether the reports continued to be submitted after Heydrich's death or not?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Yes?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, the reports..

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: Was the order concerning the annihilation of the Soviet people in conformity with the policy of the German Government or the Nazi Party or was it against it? Do you understand the question?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes. One must distinguish here: The order for the liquidation came from the Fuehrer of the Reich, and it was to be carried out by the Reichsfuehrer SS Himmler.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: But was it in conformity with the policy conducted by the Nazi Party and the German Government, or was it in contradiction to it?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: A policy amounts to a practice so that in this respect it was a policy laid down by the Fuehrer. If you were to ask whether this activity was in conformity with the idea of National Socialism, then I should say "no."

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Gen. Nikitchenko)
turns.text: I am talking about the practice.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I understood you to say that objects of value were taken from the Jewish victims by the Jewish Council of Elders.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Did the Jewish Council of Elders settle who were to be killed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The Jewish Council of Elders determined who was a Jew, and then registered the Jews individually.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: And when they registered them did they take their valuables from them?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That was done in various ways. As far as I remember, the Council of Elders was given the order to collect valuables at the same time.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: So that the Jewish Council of Elders would not know whether or not they were to be killed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: We will adjourn now until 5 minutes past 2.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: When you spoke of the written agreement between the leaders of the Einsatz groups and the Army, do you know whether or not the functions and purposes of the Einsatz groups were described in the agreement? Did the agreement say what the groups were going to do?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I no longer remember that. In any case the task of liquidation was not mentioned.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Do you understand the question?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes. I cannot quite remember whether there was a general clause in the agreement about the tasks and activities of the Security Police in the operational area, but I am certain that it contained nothing regarding the task of liquidation.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: You stated that there had been a general order for the liquidation of all Jews. Was that order in writing?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Do you know who gave the order?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Is this question with regard to the activities of the Einsatzgruppen?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Regarding the Einsatzgruppen, the order came first via Himmler, Heydrich, and Streckenbach to the Einsatzgruppen and then was repeated a second time by Himmler personally.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did a similar order go to the Army?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I know of no such order to the Army in this form.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Now do any of the defendants' counsel wish to cross-examine this witness?

turns.speaker: DR. OTTO NELTE (Counsel for Defendant Keitel)
turns.text: Witness, you said that several weeks before the opening of the Russian campaign, there were conferences regarding the tasks of the Einsatzgruppen and the Einsatzkommandos. Were you personally present at these conferences?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: May I briefly correct this by saying that the main subject was not the tasks of the Einsatzgruppen but the set-up within the operational area...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Wait a moment. Will you repeat that, please?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: May I make a correction by saying that, according to my recollection, the main subject was not the tasks of the Einsatzgruppen but the establishment of these mobile organizational units for activities within the operational area of the Army.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: In other words, this concerned tasks within the sphere of the Army?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: You testified that the written agreement was concluded between the RSHA on the one hand and the OKW and OKH on the other. Are you familiar with the difference in authority between the OKW and the OKH?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes. DR. NELTE Who was present from the OKW at these conferences?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot mention any one name because I personally was not present at these conferences, but these conferences were conducted by Heydrich on the one hand and by his deputy, Schellenberg, on the other.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Schellenberg also spoke on this question in an affidavit presented here, but he mentioned Quartermaster General Wagner as the official with whom he had to deal. Can you remember now whether this was also the case at the conferences to which you are referring?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: At any rate the name of Quartermaster General Wagner is one of the few names mentioned which I remember in connection with these conferences.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Is it known to you that Quartermaster General Wagner had nothing to do with the OKW as an institution?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: I take it that you cannot therefore name any personality who might be regarded as representative of the OKW?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, I cannot. I merely said that I remembered- that is, I still have in my mind's eye-the letterhead OKW-OKH. I took this double heading to mean that essential negotiations with Canaris were probably being carried out, that arrangements with Canaris were therefore included in this agreement, and that this accounted for the letterhead OKH plus OKW, which, to me as welt had appeared unusual, since the OKH, per se, was naturally in charge of all movement and supply.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: A joint letterhead OKW-OKH, as such, did not, of course, exist. In your case then it could have been only a typewritten copy?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I can still visualize a mimeographed sheet. 3 Jan.46

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Do you know which signatures were on this document which you visualize?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot remember, I am sorry.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: One of the judges already put the question that orders would naturally result from an agreement of this kind. Is the name of the OKW, or the signature perhaps, included in any one such order?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Now I do not understand what kind of orders you mean.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: When an agreement is made between two different organizations such as the RSHA on the one hand and, shall we say, the OKH on the other, then the office entrusted with the execution of that which has been agreed upon must be informed thereof in a form known as an "order" in military parlance. Is such an order known to you as originating from the OKW?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Please understand that no such orders from the War Office or the OKW were received by me. I should have had only orders or wishes expressed by the Army.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: By the Army or by your superior command?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No. I am speaking now..: If I think of the Armed Forces...

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: Therefore, there was no connection of any kind between you, as leader of the Einsatzgruppe, and the OKW as such?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No immediate connection. I know very well that individual reports reached the OKW through official channels.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: If you know that, can you tell me to which office? Because, after all, OKW covered a great many.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I should assume they eventually reached Canaris.

turns.speaker: DR. NELTE
turns.text: I thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. EGON KUBUSCHOK (Counsel for the Reich Cabinet)
turns.text: Witness, in your position as Chief of the SD, you will probably have some idea about the trustworthiness of the members of the Reich Cabinet and about the secrecy in which very important matters were kept. Please answer this question: whether the order which has been discussed today regarding the liquidations, in your opinion, originated in the Reich Cabinet and whether this order, in your opinion, was made known to the individual members of the Reich Cabinet?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I am convinced that both questions are to be answered in the negative.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: I should like to ask the witness a few more questions on behalf of the Defendant Speer, since counsel for the Defendant Speer is absent and I, as a colleague, have taken over this task. Witness, is it known to you that the Defendant Speer, contrary to Hitler's orders, took measures to prevent the destruction of industrial and other installations?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: That these measures also extended beyond the interior of Germany to the then still-occupied area of Upper Silesia, et alla ?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I believe that the date when I learned about this was so late that, although applicable to some small areas in the West, it no longer applied to any area in the East.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: One more question which you might perhaps know about. Do you know that the Defendant Speer prepared an attempt on Hitler's life in the middle of February of this year?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Do you know that Speer undertook to turn Himmler over to the Allies so that he could be called to account and possibly clear others who were innocent?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: This question will perhaps be answered in the affirmative by another witness. Are you well informed regarding the events of the 20th of July?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: To a considerable extent.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Is it known to you that the circle of plotters of 20 July had also planned to keep the Defendant Speer as head of his Ministry?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Do you know any details about that?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: From the participants in the plot of the 20th of July I merely learned that they had considered him, on a drafted organizational scheme, as continuing in his post as head of the armament ministry.

turns.speaker: DR. KUBUSCHOK
turns.text: Witness, do you believe that this intention of the plotters of the 20th of July was due to the fact that the Defendant Speer, in view of his activities, was considered not only in these circles but even elsewhere merely as an expert and not as a politician?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The question is very hard to answer. It is very difficult not to be considered a politician if one has been so closely connected with those authorities of the Reich who made the final political decisions and has perhaps been an essential contributor to the suggestions and proposals from which the decisions evolved. On the other hand, Minister Speer was known or believed not to be purely a politician.

turns.speaker: DR. RUDOLF MERKEL (Counsel for the Gestapo)
turns.text: Witness, do you know that in April 1933 the Gestapo was created in Prussia?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I do not know the month, but I do know the year.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know what was the purpose of creating this institution?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: To fight political opponents potentially dangerous to the State.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know how this institution, which was intended originally for Prussia only, was extended to the rest of the Reich?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Either in 1933 or in 1934 the institution of the Political Police was created in all of the Lander. These Political Police agencies were officially subordinated in 1934, as far as I remember, to the Reichsfuehrer SS as Political Police Chief of the Lander. The Prussian Secret State Police Office represented the first central headquarters. After the creation of the Main Office of the Security Police the command tasks were delegated by Himmler to Heydrich who carried them out through the Main Office of the Security Police.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Who created and instituted the Gestapo in the individual Lander?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot give you an answer to this question.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know whether before 1933, in the area which then constituted the Reich, there had existed a similar institution, a political police force?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, that existed, as far as I remember, at Police headquarters in Berlin, for instance; and I believe it was Department IA. At any rate political police organizations did exist.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know anything about the sphere of activities of this organization which existed before 1933?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes. They were the same; at any rate their activities were fundamentally the same.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know anything about the personnel of the Gestapo, which on the whole, was a new institution and consequently not constituted merely by a transfer of personnel already in existence.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: When I became acquainted with the State Police it was certainly true that the nucleus of expert personnel had been taken from the Criminal Police and the majority of the leading men in the State Police offices, that is, in the regional offices of the State Police, had risen from the ranks of the civil administration, possibly also from the Police administrations of the various Lander (Landerpolizeiverwaltungen), and that they had, in part, even been detailed from the civil administration. The same was also true for the experts within Amt IV-the Gestapo.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: You say the majority of the officials were detailed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I did not say the majority were detailed, but I said "in part."

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Detailed in part? Was it possible for any of these members of the Gestapo to resist being taken over into the Gestapo if they did not wish it, or was it not?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I would not affirm that a definite resistance was possible. Some of them might have succeeded, by cunning, in avoiding it had they not wanted to go. But if one was detailed to such an office from the civil administration, then, as an official, one simply had to obey. As an official one had to.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: The members of the Gestapo evidently consisted almost exclusively, or exclusively, of civil service officials? Do you know anything about that?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That probably was no longer the case during the war. But as a rule it should be assumed that they were officials insofar as the specialists were concerned. Some of them, of course, while in training, were not yet officials and others again were engaged merely as employees or, especially, as assistants.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Can you tell me the approximate number of the members of the Gestapo towards the end of the war?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I estimate the total organization of the Gestapo, including the regional offices and the occupied territories, at about 30,000.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: There was therefore within the Gestapo a considerable percentage of officials who were merely administrative officials and had nothing to do with operational functions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, of course.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: And what was the percentage of these administrative officials who performed purely administrative functions?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: We must, in the first instance, take into consideration that this number included the assistants, as well as the women; and I cannot give you any figures off hand. But it is certain that a proportion of one specialist to three or four persons not employed in a functional capacity could not be considered excessive.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Do you know anything about who was responsible for the direction and administration of the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: It was Obergruppenfuehrer Pohl.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Did or did not the Gestapo have anything to do with the direction and with the administration of the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: According to my knowledge, not.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Therefore, no members of the Gestapo were active or in any way involved in the measures carried out in the concentration camps?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: As far as I could judge from a distance, only investigating officials of the State Police were active in the concentration camps.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Did the Gestapo in any way participate in the mass executions undertaken by your Einsatzgruppe which you described this morning?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Only to the same extent as every other person present in the Einsatzgruppe.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: I ask the Tribunal to give me the opportunity of questioning this witness again after the return of the Defendant Kaltenbrunner, since I am obliged to rely exclusively on information received from Kaltenbrunner.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I think that the Tribunal will be prepared to allow you to put further questions at a later stage.

turns.speaker: DR. MERKEL
turns.text: Thank you. PROFESSOR DR. FRANZ EXNER (Counsel for the General Staff and the High Command of the German Armed Forces): Witness, you mentioned the negotiations which took place in the OKW, which later led to an agreement between OKW and OKH on the one side, and the Reich Security Main Office (RSHA) on the other. I am interested in this point: Can you assert that during the negotiations for this agreement there was mention of the extermination and the killing of Jews?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot say anything concrete on this particular subject, but I do not believe it.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: You do not believe it?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: In addition you have told us that the Commanding General of the 11th Army knew about the liquidations, and I should like to ask you: Do you know anything regarding the commanding generals of the other armies?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In general they must have been informed through the speech of the Fuehrer before the beginning of the Russian campaign.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: That is a conclusion that you have drawn?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No, it is not a conclusion that I have drawn; it is merely a report on the contents of the speech which, according to Himmler's statement, Hitler had made to the commanding generals.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: Now you have spoken about directives given by the Commanding General of the 11th Army. What kind of directives were they?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I first spoke about the commanding general in the Nikolaiev incident, that is, about the order given at that time that the liquidations should take place 200 kilometers away from the headquarters of the High Command of the army. The second time, I did not speak about the commanding general of the army but about the High Command of the army at Simferopol, because I cannot say, with any certainty, who had requested the competent Einsatzkommando at Simferopol to speed up the liquidations.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: That is the very question I should like to put to you: With whom in the 11th Army did you negotiate at that time?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I, personally, did not negotiate at all with anyone on this subject, as I was not the person directly concerned with these matters; but the High Command of the Army negotiated with the competent local Einsatzkommando either through the responsible army office, which at all times was in touch with the Einsatzkommandos, namely the I-C or the I-CAD, or else through the staff of the CQ.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: Who gave you orders for the advance?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: The orders for the advance came, as a rule, from the Chief of Staff.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: From the Chief of Staff? The Commanding General of the army at the time referred to was Von Manstein. In this case was there ever an order signed by Von Manstein?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I cannot remember any such order; but when the advance was being discussed there were oral consultations with Von Manstein, the Chief of Staff, and me.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: When the advance was being discussed?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: You said that the Army was opposed to these liquidations. Can you state how this became evident?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Not the Army, but the leaders were inwardly opposed to the liquidations.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: Yes; but I mean, how did you recognize that fact?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In our conversations. Not only the leaders of the Army were opposed to the liquidations but also most of those who had to carry them out.

turns.speaker: DR. EXNER
turns.text: I thank you.

turns.speaker: PROFESSOR DR. HERBERT KRAUS (Counsel for Defendant Schacht)
turns.text: Were you acquainted with the personal records on Reichsbank President Schacht kept in your department?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Do you know why, after the 20th of July 1944, the former Reichsbank President Schacht was arrested and interned in a concentration camp?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Probably the occasion of the 20th of July was favorable also for the conviction at last of Reichsbank President Schacht, who was known to be inimical to the Party, inasmuch as by means of witnesses or other methods he could be brought to trial in connection with the events of the 20th of July.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Then Defendant Schacht was known to your people as being inimical to the Party? OUT ENCORE Yes, at least from 1937-1938 on.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Since the year 1937 or 1938? And you also suspected him of participating in Putsche?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Personally I did not suspect this, because I was not concerned with these matters at all He was mainly under suspicion because of his well-known enmity. But, as far as I know, this suspicion was never confirmed.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Can you tell me, who caused Schacht to be arrested?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: That I cannot say.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Then you don't know whether the arrest was ordered by the Fuehrer, by Himmler, or by some subordinate authority.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I don't think the order could possibly have come from any subordinate authority.

turns.speaker: DR. KRAUS
turns.text: Then you assume that it had been ordered by the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: At least by Himmler.

turns.speaker: DR. OTTO STAHMER (Counsel for Defendant Goering)
turns.text: Witness, if I understood you correctly, you said: At the beginning of 1933, after the seizure of power by Hitler, the Gestapo was created in Prussia; but before that time there had already existed in Prussia an organization with similar tasks, for instance at the Police headquarters in Berlin with Department IA, with the difference that this organization was opposed to National Socialism, whereas now the contrary is true. But its task was likewise to keep political opponents under observation and if need be to arrest them, and thus to protect the State from these political opponents.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: You said further that in 1933, after the seizure of power, a political police with identical tasks was also instituted in all the other Lander.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: Yes, in the year 1933-1934.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: This political police, which existed in the various Lander, was then centralized in 1934 and its direction handed over to Himmler?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: It was not at first centralized, but Himmler did become Chief of Police of all the Lander.

turns.speaker: DR. STAHMER
turns.text: Now one more question. Did the Prussian Gestapo play a leading role, as far as the other Lander were concerned, as early as 1933 or only after Himmler took over the leadership in 1934?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I do not believe that the Prussian State Police, which after all was under the leadership of Reich Marshal Goering, became, at that time, the competent authority for the other Lander as well.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER OTTO KRANZBUEHLER (Counsel for Defendant Doenitz)
turns.text: I am speaking as the representative of the counsel for Defendant Grossadmiral Raeder. [Turning to the.witness.] Witness, you just mentioned a speech of the Fuehrer before the army commanders, in which the Fuehrer is supposed to have given instructions to the commanders regarding the liquidation of Jews. Which conference do you mean.

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: A conference which must have taken place shortly before the Russian campaign with the commanders of the army groups and the armies at the Fuehrer's quarters.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were the commanders of the various branches of the Armed Forces absent?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I do not know that.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Were you yourself present at this conference?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No. I have recounted this conference on the basis of a conversation I had with Himmler.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did this conversation with Himmler take place in a large circle of people or was it a private conversation?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: It was a private conversation.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Did you have the impression that Himmler stated facts, or do you consider it possible that he wished to encourage you in your difficult task?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: No. The conversation took place much, much later and did not spring from such motives, but from resentment at the attitude of certain generals of the Armed Forces. Himmler wanted to say that these generals of the Armed Forces could not disassociate themselves from the events that had taken place, as they were just as responsible as all the rest.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: And when did this conversation with Himmler take place?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: In May 1945, at Flensburg.

turns.speaker: FLOTTENRICHTER KRANZBUEHLER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Witness, with regard to the command channels at the disposal of the RSHA for the execution of its orders and measures and for the transmission of these orders to tactical organizations, such as the SD and the concentration camps, did the RSHA possess its own command channels or did it rely on the channels of the Leadership Corps organization, that is, were these orders forwarded via the Gauleitung and the Kreisleitung?

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
turns.text: I know nothing about it. I consider it entirely out of the question.

turns.speaker: DR.SERVATIUS
turns.text: You consider it entirely out of the question that the Gauleitung and the Kreisleitung had been informed? How was it, for instance...

turns.speaker: OHLENDORF
